title: 145 Tests, 88% Coverage: How One Person Systematically Improves an AI Agent Project
summary: suri-counsel is an ISB high school counselor agent built for my daughter. 125 tests passing green, but zero coverage visibility. Starting from pytest-cov, battling litellm namespace package bugs, splitting counselor.py into 3 files, and adding full A11y support — ending at 145 tests / 88% coverage.
body:
## From "All Green" to "Truly Green"

suri-counsel's CI dashboard had been green for weeks. 125 tests, all passing.

Then I ran `pytest --cov` and saw `llm.py` at **16%** coverage. This file is the entire agent's LLM calling layer — retry logic, DashScope fallback, JSON parsing — the core of the core, almost completely untested.

All green doesn't mean all covered. Classic false confidence trap.

## The Five-Step Plan

The improvement wasn't ad hoc. First, run coverage to see blind spots. Then prioritize by ROI:

| # | Task | Value | Effort |
|---|------|-------|--------|
| 1 | pytest-cov integration | See blind spots | Small |
| 2 | llm.py unit tests | Core dependency at 0 tests | Medium |
| 3 | counselor.py split | Clean architecture | Medium |
| 4 | A11y baseline | Usability | Medium |
| 5 | Auth minor fix | Polish | Small |

Key discovery: Auth was actually much better than initially assessed (PBKDF2-SHA256 with 310k iterations + JWT + production-enforced auth). The real priorities were test coverage and code architecture.

## Step 1: Making Coverage Visible

Added `pytest-cov` to `requirements-dev.txt`, configured `pyproject.toml`:

```toml
[tool.pytest.ini_options]
addopts = "--cov=server --cov-report=term-missing"
```

First run results:

```
server/llm.py        16%   ← biggest hole
server/counselor.py  94%   ← better than expected
server/plan.py       70%   ← some paths uncovered
Overall              83%
```

The value of coverage numbers isn't chasing 100% — it's **making blind spots visible**. The 16% on llm.py immediately became the target for Step 2.

## Step 2: The litellm Namespace Package Trap

Writing tests for `llm.py`, the very first `@patch("litellm.completion")` exploded:

```
AttributeError: <module 'litellm' (namespace)> does not have the attribute 'completion'
```

litellm v1.82.6 installs without `__init__.py`, making Python treat it as a namespace package. `litellm.completion` doesn't exist as an attribute, so `@patch` can't work.

**First attempt**: Changed patch target to `server.llm.litellm.completion` — still failed because it references the same namespace module object.

**Final solution**: An autouse fixture that manually injects the attribute:

```python
@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)
def _mock_litellm():
    import litellm
    if not hasattr(litellm, "completion"):
        litellm.completion = MagicMock()
        yield
        delattr(litellm, "completion")
    else:
        yield
```

The core idea: if the module lacks the attribute, give it a stub so `@patch` can swap normally. Clean up after the test.

20 new tests covering retry, DashScope fallback, JSON fence cleanup, tool-based calls — all paths. llm.py coverage went from 16% to **95%**.

## Step 3: 210 Lines Into Three Files

`counselor.py` was the agent's brain — profile detection, system prompt building, LLM message formatting, tool loop engine, session management. 210 lines isn't massive, but five responsibilities in one file means holding everything in your head for every change.

Split:

```
counselor.py         → entry + orchestration (54 lines)
counselor_prompt.py  → system prompt building (47 lines)
counselor_tools.py   → tool loop engine (77 lines)
```

The split itself wasn't hard. The hard part was **not breaking existing tests**. 14 test files had 68+ mock patch targets pointing at `server.counselor.llm_chat_with_tools`, `server.counselor.execute_tool`, etc. Once the module splits, all those targets break.

The fix was mechanical but had to be precise: global sed replacement across 14 files, changing `server.counselor.*` to `server.counselor_tools.*`. Miss one and you get silent test failures.

Post-split: 145 tests passing, each file under 80 lines, clean responsibilities.

## Step 4: A11y Is Not Polish

This is an app for my 13-year-old daughter. She doesn't need a screen reader, but good A11y practices mean better keyboard navigation, clearer semantic structure, better UX.

Four core components changed:

**App.jsx** — sidebar navigation:
- `<div onClick>` → `<button>` + `aria-current="page"`
- `<span onClick>` → `<button>` + `aria-label`
- `<div>` → `<aside aria-label="主导航">`
- Added Escape key handler for mobile sidebar

**ChatMessages.jsx** — chat area:
- Container gets `role="log"` + `aria-live="polite"`
- Each message gets `role="article"` + `aria-label`
- Typing indicator gets `role="status"`

**ChatInputBar.jsx** — input area:
- All icon buttons get `aria-label`
- Hidden file input gets `aria-hidden="true" tabIndex={-1}`

Converting `<div onClick>` to `<button>` requires CSS reset (background: none, border: none, width: 100%) — otherwise button defaults break the layout.

## Step 5: One Line of Auth

```python
TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 24 * 60 * 60  # 24 hours (was 7 days)
```

Personal project — 7 days is fine. But 24 hours is the better default. Smaller window if a token leaks.

## Final Results

- **145/145 tests passing** (20 new llm.py tests)
- **Coverage 83% → 88%**
- **llm.py 16% → 95%**
- **counselor.py 210 lines → 3 files under 80 lines each**
- **Full A11y baseline**: semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard nav
- **Frontend compiles clean**

One commit, 794 insertions / 259 deletions.

## Reflection

Biggest lesson: **Coverage numbers matter not for the numbers, but for making blind spots visible.** Without pytest-cov, llm.py's 16% stays invisible forever. And the litellm namespace package issue — if not for writing tests, it would never surface.

Second lesson: **The cost of module splitting isn't the split itself — it's updating all references.** 68 mock patch targets took more time than writing the split code. That's why test coverage comes first — it's both the improvement metric and the refactoring safety net.
